nativeRange.habitat: Shallow protected lagoons and inshore reefs; silty bottoms with coral and rubble; depth 1–18 m

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on live harpacticoid copepods, amphipods, small gastropods, polychaete worms, and ostracods. Wild-caught specimens almost always refuse prepared or frozen foods; captive-bred individuals may accept frozen Mysis shrimp or baby brine shrimp over time. A thriving pod population in the display tank and refugium is essential. Tank should be established for at least 6–8 months before introduction.

avoidWith: competitive feeders (gobies, Dottybacks, Pseudochromis, Damselfish, Anthias, Basslets)

Product page for Mandarin Dragonet; product photography is all-rights-reserved. Included as tertiary fallback only.

summary: One of the ocean's most vividly coloured fish, the Green Mandarin is a reef-safe dragonet requiring an established tank with a thriving copepod population — suitable for expert saltwater keepers only.
careNotes:
The Green Mandarin (Synchiropus splendidus) is widely considered one of the most challenging marine fish to keep long-term due to its highly specialised diet. In the wild it continuously grazes on tiny live prey (copepods, amphipods, polychaete worms, small gastropods) throughout the day. In captivity, wild-caught specimens almost never accept prepared or frozen foods, making starvation the primary cause of death. A minimum tank age of 6–8 months is recommended to allow a self-sustaining copepod population to develop; an attached refugium dramatically improves survivability by acting as a continuous pod breeding ground. Captive-bred individuals are significantly easier to feed and may be trained to accept frozen Mysis shrimp or baby brine shrimp.

Males are distinguishable by a prominent elongated first dorsal fin spine. Only one male should be kept per display — they will fight aggressively. A single male can be kept with one or more females. Males can be kept in mated pairs; spawning in captivity is possible and occurs with pairs ascending toward the surface, releasing up to ~200 eggs per event.

The species is notoriously disease-resistant when eating well, as its body is covered in a thick, foul-smelling mucus (pheromone-laden) that deters ectoparasites. It lacks scales entirely.

Tank size disagreement noted: BlueZoo Aquatics recommends 75 gallons (~284 L) minimum to sustain adequate live prey populations; some sources (e.g., Aquariadise) cite 30 gallons as the minimum. The conservative 75-gallon figure is used here because pod availability is the limiting factor, not territory. For a mated pair, 60+ gallons is advised by most sources.

Temperature cross-source note: FishBase wild-observation range is 25–29.3 °C; aquarium-specific sources recommend 24–27 °C as optimal tank range. The narrower tank range is used.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning is achievable but uncommon outside specialised breeding setups. A mated male–female pair will typically spawn in the evening, with both fish ascending together toward the water surface to release eggs and sperm simultaneously. Up to ~200 pelagic eggs are released per spawning event; events may repeat weekly with an established pair. Larvae are extremely small and require rotifers and then copepod nauplii as first foods. Raising larvae to juvenile stage is advanced-level; documented by a small number of specialist breeders.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source confirmed care level (difficult), temperament (peaceful), reef-safe status, and dietary requirements. Species-level detail page returned 404; dragonet collection page used.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-verified adult size (2.5 in / ~6.4 cm), minimum tank size (75 gal / 284 L), temperament, reef compatibility, diet (live copepods/amphipods), native range (Indo-Pacific), care level (expert-only), and species to avoid. BlueZoo calls care level 'expert-only' vs LiveAquaria 'difficult'; conservative 'expert' used.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Callionymidae / Callionymiformes), maximum length 7.0 cm TL, wild temperature range 25–29.3 °C, native range (Western Pacific: Ryukyu Islands to Australia), and habitat (shallow lagoons/inshore reefs, silty bottoms with coral and rubble, 1–18 m depth).

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Additional cross-check for pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), tank temperature (23.9–27.2 °C), lifespan (5 years captivity / 15 years wild), compatible tankmates, and breeding notes. Minor disagreement on min tank size (Aquariadise: 30 gal) vs BlueZoo (75 gal); conservative 75-gal value used.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning possible with a mated pair; larvae require rotifers then copepod nauplii. Advanced-level rearing.
